title: practical english usage - Part 173
content:
At the beginning of a clause, only nobody, nothing, etc are used.

Don't tell them anything.  Your suggestion won't help either of us.

I opened the door, but I couldn't see anybody, (     .. .-but I eould negative meaning.

not themselves negative words (►   222) - they have to be used with not to give a anybody, not anything, not ever, etc. Note that anybody, anything, ever, etc are Nobody, nothing, never, etc are rather emphatic. We   often prefer to use not 2  nobody         and   not    anybody,         etc not I've never understood what she wants, (     I haven't never-understood-.. .) not Nothing matters now - everything's finished, (     Nothing doesn't matter...) not Your suggestion will help neither of us. (     .. .won't help neither...) not Tell them nothing, (     Don't tell them nothing.) not (     ... but I couldn't-see nobody;) I opened the door, but I couldn't see anybody / I could see nobody.

normally enough.

accompanying pronouns or adverbs. In standard English, one negative word is In some languages, a negative idea is expressed both by the verb and by 1  English     and   other     languages 220 multiple      negatives:      I couldn't        see    nobody.

Basic Clause Types    Section  19 grammar •  221   ambiguous negatives Because Amy  was there, I didn't sing.

shown clearly by putting the because-clause at the beginning.

'My  reason for singing was not that Amy  was there’ . The  first meaning could be This sentence could mean 'My  reason for not singing was that Amy  was there’  or I didn't sing because Amy  was there.

Negative sentences with because-clauses are often ambiguous.

It wasn't the car crash that killed him. (Only one possible meaning.) The  car crash didn't kill him. (Did he live, or did something else kill him?) Compare: sometimes arise. They  can usually be avoided by reorganising the sentence.

writing it is usually clear from the context and situation. However, confusions The  exact meaning is shown in speech by stress and intonation, and even in Adam didn't write to Emma  yesterday - he wrote this morning.

Adam didn't write to Emma  yesterday - he wrote to Anna.

Adam didn't write to Emma  yesterday - he phoned her.

In a negative structure, not can refer to different parts of a sentence. Compare: 221 ambiguous        negatives       _ _______ (= ... whether I ought to ...) I wonder whether I oughtn't to go and see a doctor - I'm feeling a bit funny.

I shouldn't be surprised if they didn't get married soon. (= ... if they got used after expressions of doubt or uncertainty.

In informal speech, a negative verb (without a negative meaning) is sometimes 6 extra    negative       in  expressions         of  doubt We won't be back before midnight, I don't suppose.

She hasn't got much  chance of passing the exam, I don't think.

difference to the meaning of the statement.

added after negative statements. In this case, the extra negative makes no In informal speech, expressions like I don't think or I don't suppose are often 5 .../  don't     think,      etc For more information about ain't, ►    337.4.

nobody no time. (American song by Bert Williams) I ain't never done nothing to nobody, and I ain't never got nothing from I ain't seen nobody. (Standard English: I haven't seen anybody.) with a single negative meaning.

In many British, American and other dialects, two or more negatives can be used 4 dialects grammar •  222  non-affirmative words: anybody, ever, yet, etc in question For information about particular non-affirmative words, check  in the Index to find the entries for the words Few people have ever seen her laugh.

It's difficult to understand anything he says.

I'd rather do it without anybody's help.

Please forget that I ever told you anything about it.

He denied that he had ever seen her.

I've hardly been anywhere since Christmas.

He seldom says anything.

She writes better than anybody I know.

I wonder if she found anything.

Let me know if you have any trouble.

prepositions, adjectives and determiners that have a negative kind of meaning.
